catalyst.source: The novel composite photocatalyst of trimodal porous silica (TPS)g-C 3 N 4 nanotubes was fabricated via a twostep hydrothermal synthesis method with intermediate pretreatment.
catalyst.context: The novel composite photocatalyst of trimodal porous silica (TPS)g-C 3 N 4 nanotubes was fabricated via a twostep hydrothermal synthesis method with intermediate pretreatment.The TPS was extracted from rice husk, which is agricultural waste, to further reduce the cost of photocatalyst preparation.A series of characterization results, including X-ray diffraction, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, scanning electron microscopy, N 2 adsorption-desorption, and pore size analysis, illustrated the properties of crystal, pore structure, and morphology of the composite photocatalyst.The TPSg-C 3 N 4 presented a hollow tubular shape with a large surface area and CO 2 adsorption capacity, so there were more catalytic active reaction sites.The photocatalytic activity measurement was operated, and only ethanol was detected when g-C 3 N 4 NT and composite TGNT were used as photocatalysts.In particular, the ethanol yield of TGNT2 was 196 mg, and after 10 cycles of running, the photocatalytic activity decreased by less than 5.UV-Vis diffuse reflectance spectra, photoluminescence spectra, and photoelectrochemical analysis revealed the mechanism of the photocatalytic activity enhancement from the aspects of visible light absorption, photo-generated electronhole pair separation and transfer, and charges recombination.It theoretically explained the prospect of this fabrication strategy.It demonstrated that TGNT is a low-cost, stable, efficient, and promising photocatalyst to reduce CO 2 to ethanol under visible light.

catalyst.source: The photocatalytic activity measurement was operated, and only ethanol was detected when g-C 3 N 4 NT and composite TGNT were used as photocatalysts.

catalyst.source: In summary, the TPSg-C 3 N 4 nanotube composite photocatalyst was successfully prepared by a two-step hydrothermal method, which is a promising photocatalyst for reducing CO 2 to ethanol.
catalyst.context: In summary, the TPSg-C 3 N 4 nanotube composite photocatalyst was successfully prepared by a two-step hydrothermal method, which is a promising photocatalyst for reducing CO 2 to ethanol.The crystal structure and chemical composition of g-C 3 N 4 NT are maintained, but the loading of TPS caused TGNT to improve the specific surface area and increase the reaction sites.Based on the investigations of UV-Vis DRS, PL, photocurrent responses, and EIS, the mechanism of improving the photocatalytic activity of TGNT composites was confirmed.Appropriate loading of TPS can increase the visible light-trapping ability, improve reduction potential and separation-transfer efficiency of photo-generated charges, reduce the charge transfer resistance, and inhibit hole-electron pair recombination.However, excessive loading of TPS enhances charge recombination.Therefore, the performance of TGNT2 in the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 is enhanced, reaching 196 molg.After several cycles of photocatalytic activity and photocurrent measurement, the reproducibility proves its excellent working stability. Fig. 1.Powder XRD patterns of as-prepared photocatalysts. Fig. 2. FTIR spectra of as-prepared photocatalysts. Fig. 3. (a) X-ray photoelectron survey spectra.High-resolution spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) O 1s, (e) Si s2p, and (f) VB spectra of as-prepared photocatalysts. Fig. 4. SEM images of (a) MCA, (b) (c) g-C 3 N 4 NT, (d) TGNT1, (e) TGNT2, and (f) TGNT3.TEM images of (g) TGNT1, (h) TGNT2, and (i) TGNT3. Fig. 5. (a) N 2 adsorption-desorption isotherms at -196 C, (b) pore size distribution, and (c) CO 2 adsorption isotherms of g-C 3 N 4 NT, TGNT, and TPS. .Wang et al. Fig. 6.(a) UV-Vis diffuse reflectance spectra and (b) estimated band gap of g-C 3 N 4 NT, TGNT, and bulk g-C 3 N 4 . Fig. 7. (a) Ethanol yield, (b) methanol and ethanol evolution rate of as-prepared photocatalysts, (c) GC-MS spectra of the product using 13 CO 2 as carbon source, and (d) photocatalytic reduction cycle runs of CO 2 to ethanol over the TGNT2. Fig. 8. (a) Photoluminescence spectra, (b) photocurrent responses, (c) EIS Nyquist plots, and (d) schematic diagram of the electronic band structure of as-prepared photocatalysts. Texture properties of g-C 3 N 4 NT, TGNT, and TPS.The CO 2 adsorption isotherms of the samples at 0 C are shown in Fig. 5(c) and Fig. S6(b).The CO 2 adsorption quantity is consistent with the trend of N 2 adsorption quantity and the pore volume, indicating that loading TPS can increase the CO 2 physical adsorption sites.The CO 2 adsorption capacity of TGNT2 and TGNT3 are significantly increased, reaching 13.67 and 20.79 cm 3 g.UV-Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of g-C 3 N 4 NT, TGNT, and bulk g-C 3 N 4 are shown in Fig. This work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51904073), the National Key Research and Development, China Project (No. 2019YFC1905200), the Natural Science Foundation of Liaoning Province, China (No. 2020-BS-053), and the Postdoctoral Research Foundation of Northeastern University in China (No. 20190304). The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper. Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at https:doi.org10.1016j.cej.2021.130877.

light source.source: It demonstrated that TGNT is a low-cost, stable, efficient, and promising photocatalyst to reduce CO 2 to ethanol under visible light.
light source.context: The novel composite photocatalyst of trimodal porous silica (TPS)g-C 3 N 4 nanotubes was fabricated via a twostep hydrothermal synthesis method with intermediate pretreatment.The TPS was extracted from rice husk, which is agricultural waste, to further reduce the cost of photocatalyst preparation.A series of characterization results, including X-ray diffraction, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, scanning electron microscopy, N 2 adsorption-desorption, and pore size analysis, illustrated the properties of crystal, pore structure, and morphology of the composite photocatalyst.The TPSg-C 3 N 4 presented a hollow tubular shape with a large surface area and CO 2 adsorption capacity, so there were more catalytic active reaction sites.The photocatalytic activity measurement was operated, and only ethanol was detected when g-C 3 N 4 NT and composite TGNT were used as photocatalysts.In particular, the ethanol yield of TGNT2 was 196 mg, and after 10 cycles of running, the photocatalytic activity decreased by less than 5.UV-Vis diffuse reflectance spectra, photoluminescence spectra, and photoelectrochemical analysis revealed the mechanism of the photocatalytic activity enhancement from the aspects of visible light absorption, photo-generated electronhole pair separation and transfer, and charges recombination.It theoretically explained the prospect of this fabrication strategy.It demonstrated that TGNT is a low-cost, stable, efficient, and promising photocatalyst to reduce CO 2 to ethanol under visible light.

light source.source: A 300 W Xe lamp (Microsolar300, PerfectLight, China) equipped with a cutoff filter ( 420 nm) was used as the visible light source to illuminate the reactant through the quartz window.
light source.context: Melamine, Polyethylene glycol (PEG) 400, cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), ammonia aqueous, NaOH, KHCO 3 , and Na 2 SO 3 were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent.All reagents used in this work were analytically pure and used without further purification.Sodium silicate solution was extracted from rice husk by the alkali solution method.Briefly, 38 g of calcined rice husk ash and 10 g of NaOH were mixed in 350 mL of water, and then stirred and refluxed at 70 C for 24 h.The mixed liquid was filtered, and the Si content in the clear liquid measured by ICP-OES was 39.1 gL. The g-C 3 N 4 nanotubes (NT) were prepared by a two-step process with a hydrothermal performing process.Firstly, 2 g of melamine was dissolved into 120 mL of deionized water under the condition of stirring at 80 C for 10 min.Then, the obtained clear solution was transferred to a 200 mL PTFE-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated to 180 C for 24 h.The crystalline product, preformed white melamine-cyanuric acid (MCA), was collected by filtered, washed several times with water, and completely dried.Secondly, the 3 g of MCA precursor was placed in an alumina crucible with a cover and then calcined at 550 C for h in a tube furnace with N 2 gas flow.For comparison, the bulk g-C 3 N 4 was prepared by thermal polymerization of melamine.Typically, 2 g of melamine was heated at 550 C for 4 h.The light yellow powder was collected after cooled and ground. The TPS was synthesized by the following soft template method.10 g of PEG was dissolved in 40 mL deionized water and stirred for min at 40 C, which labeled as solution A. 1 g of CTAB was dissolved in 10 mL deionized water at 60 C, which labeled as solution B. Solution A was added to solution B, and then 18 mL of sodium silicate solution was added dropwise to the mixed solution after 1 mL of ammonia aqueous was added.The mixture gel was stirred with 300 rmin at 40 C for 8 h.Finally, the product was filtrated, washed, and dried.Then the template was removed by heated to 550 C for 6 h with a ramp rate of 10 Cmin. The TPSg-C 3 N 4 NT composite was prepared by a hydrothermal method.0.3 g of g-C 3 N 4 NT was dispersed in 100 mL of water by ultrasonic treatment for 10 min.0.1 g of TPS was added to the above suspension, and the ultrasonic treatment was continued for 30 min.Then, the mixed solution was transferred to a 200 mL PTFE-lined stainless steel autoclave, which was heated at 120 C for 12 h.Finally, the product was filtrated, washed with deionized water, and dried at 80 C. Samples with the same preparation method but different amounts of TPS, 0.1 g, 0.2 g, and 0.3 g, were labeled TGNT1, TGNT2, and TGNT3. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed using a D8 Advance X-ray powder diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) with Cu K radiation ( 0.154 nm).The pattern data was recorded at 2 5 to 75 with the step size of 0.02 .Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed on a Cary 660 Spectrum One FTIR spectrometer (Agilent Technologies, Australia) in the range of 4000-600 cm -1 .X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data was obtained using an Axis Ultra DLD spectrometer (KRATOS, UK) using monochromatic Al K radiation (h1486.6eV).The signal at 284.8 eV of the C (1s) region was employed to adjust the binding energies for all of the XPS spectra.Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained on an ULTRA PLUS field emission scanning electron microscope (ZEISS, Germany) at an accelerating voltage of 15 kV.Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were observed with a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) working at 200 kV.The N 2 adsorption-desorption isotherm at -196 C and CO 2 adsorption isotherm at 0 C were measured on an ASAP 2460 static adsorption analyzer (Micromeritics, USA).Specific surface area, pore size distribution, and total pore volume were calculated by the Brunauer-Emmet-Teller (BET) method, the non-local density functional theory (NLDFT) method, and the adsorption amount at the relative pressure PP 0 of 0.995.Before each measurement, the samples were degassed at 200 C for 6 h.UV-Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) data was conducted using a Lambda 650S UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA) in the wavelength range of 200-800 nm.Photoluminescence (PL) spectra was recorded using a Fluoromax-4 spectrophotometer (HORIBA, Japan) with an excitation wavelength of 325 nm. Photocatalytic activity measurement was carried out in a homemade closed photocatalytic reactor.It can control the gas flow through a mass flow controller (AALBORG, USA), cut off the gas path through the valves, and control the reaction temperature through a chiller (YKKY, China).The experiment of stable photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 under visible light irradiation is thus realized.Before illumination, 100 mg of photocatalysts were suspended in 100 mL of KHCO 3 (0.1 M) and Na 2 SO 3 (0.1 M) under magnetic stirring in the double-layer Pyrex glass reactor, and pure CO 2 gas was continuously through the solution for 30 min to eliminate air and dissolve oxygen.KHCO 3 is used to solution to enhance the solubility of CO 2 during photocatalytic reactions, and Na 2 SO 3 act as holes sacrifice agent 36.A 300 W Xe lamp (Microsolar300, PerfectLight, China) equipped with a cutoff filter ( 420 nm) was used as the visible light source to illuminate the reactant through the quartz window.A constant temperature water tank filled with ethylene glycol aqueous solution was used to control the reaction temperature at 0 C.During the entire irradiation process, pure CO 2 was continuously bubbled through the above solution in the reactor.At a certain time interval, a syringe with a nylon membrane filter (pore size 0.45 m) was used to take out 0.5 mL of liquid product to remove the photocatalyst particles.The ethanol concentration of the 3.0 L liquid sample was analyzed by a QP2010 Ultra gas chromatography mass spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) with an AOC-20i auto-injector.The gas in the headspace of the reactor was analyzed by a 6890N gas chromatography (Agilent Technologies, USA).Under the same experimental conditions, the comparative experiment was carried out in the dark or without CO 2 gas flow.After the reaction, the sample was separated by centrifugation for repeated measurements, which was to investigate the recyclability.The 13 C isotope labeling experiment was performed to verify that the products were from CO 2 photocatalytic conversion.The 13 CO 2 was used as feed gas instead of 12 CO 2 in the reaction process, and the products were analyzed by the QP2010 Ultra GC-MS. 5 mg of the photocatalyst was dispersed in a mixed solution of 1 mL of water, 0.35 mL of ethanol, and 10 L of nafion.150 L of the sample was dropped on indium tin oxide (ITO) glass with the area of 1 0.8 cm 2 and dried in a vacuum.The photocurrent measurement was carried out on a CHI660E electrochemical analyzer (ChenHua, China).It was operated in a three-electrode system, and a platinum electrode, indium tin oxide glass, and saturated AgAgCl electrode were acted as the counter electrode, working electrode, and standard electrode, respectively.The electrolyte was a 0.1 M sodium sulfate solution.Electrochemical impendence spectroscopes (EIS) was carried out on a PP211 electrochemical analyzer (Zahner, Germany).It was performed in the presence of 5 mM K 3 Fe(CN) 6 K 4 Fe(CN) 6 (1:1) mixture as a redox probe in 0.1 M potassium chloride (KCl) at 0.24 V.

lamp.source: A 300 W Xe lamp (Microsolar300, PerfectLight, China) equipped with a cutoff filter ( 420 nm) was used as the visible light source to illuminate the reactant through the quartz window.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed using a D8 Advance X-ray powder diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) with Cu K radiation ( 0.154 nm).The pattern data was recorded at 2 5 to 75 with the step size of 0.02 .Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed on a Cary 660 Spectrum One FTIR spectrometer (Agilent Technologies, Australia) in the range of 4000-600 cm -1 .X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data was obtained using an Axis Ultra DLD spectrometer (KRATOS, UK) using monochromatic Al K radiation (h1486.6eV).The signal at 284.8 eV of the C (1s) region was employed to adjust the binding energies for all of the XPS spectra.Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained on an ULTRA PLUS field emission scanning electron microscope (ZEISS, Germany) at an accelerating voltage of 15 kV.Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were observed with a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) working at 200 kV.The N 2 adsorption-desorption isotherm at -196 C and CO 2 adsorption isotherm at 0 C were measured on an ASAP 2460 static adsorption analyzer (Micromeritics, USA).Specific surface area, pore size distribution, and total pore volume were calculated by the Brunauer-Emmet-Teller (BET) method, the non-local density functional theory (NLDFT) method, and the adsorption amount at the relative pressure PP 0 of 0.995.Before each measurement, the samples were degassed at 200 C for 6 h.UV-Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) data was conducted using a Lambda 650S UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA) in the wavelength range of 200-800 nm.Photoluminescence (PL) spectra was recorded using a Fluoromax-4 spectrophotometer (HORIBA, Japan) with an excitation wavelength of 325 nm. Photocatalytic activity measurement was carried out in a homemade closed photocatalytic reactor.It can control the gas flow through a mass flow controller (AALBORG, USA), cut off the gas path through the valves, and control the reaction temperature through a chiller (YKKY, China).The experiment of stable photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 under visible light irradiation is thus realized.Before illumination, 100 mg of photocatalysts were suspended in 100 mL of KHCO 3 (0.1 M) and Na 2 SO 3 (0.1 M) under magnetic stirring in the double-layer Pyrex glass reactor, and pure CO 2 gas was continuously through the solution for 30 min to eliminate air and dissolve oxygen.KHCO 3 is used to solution to enhance the solubility of CO 2 during photocatalytic reactions, and Na 2 SO 3 act as holes sacrifice agent 36.A 300 W Xe lamp (Microsolar300, PerfectLight, China) equipped with a cutoff filter ( 420 nm) was used as the visible light source to illuminate the reactant through the quartz window.A constant temperature water tank filled with ethylene glycol aqueous solution was used to control the reaction temperature at 0 C.During the entire irradiation process, pure CO 2 was continuously bubbled through the above solution in the reactor.At a certain time interval, a syringe with a nylon membrane filter (pore size 0.45 m) was used to take out 0.5 mL of liquid product to remove the photocatalyst particles.The ethanol concentration of the 3.0 L liquid sample was analyzed by a QP2010 Ultra gas chromatography mass spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) with an AOC-20i auto-injector.The gas in the headspace of the reactor was analyzed by a 6890N gas chromatography (Agilent Technologies, USA).Under the same experimental conditions, the comparative experiment was carried out in the dark or without CO 2 gas flow.After the reaction, the sample was separated by centrifugation for repeated measurements, which was to investigate the recyclability.The 13 C isotope labeling experiment was performed to verify that the products were from CO 2 photocatalytic conversion.The 13 CO 2 was used as feed gas instead of 12 CO 2 in the reaction process, and the products were analyzed by the QP2010 Ultra GC-MS. 5 mg of the photocatalyst was dispersed in a mixed solution of 1 mL of water, 0.35 mL of ethanol, and 10 L of nafion.150 L of the sample was dropped on indium tin oxide (ITO) glass with the area of 1 0.8 cm 2 and dried in a vacuum.The photocurrent measurement was carried out on a CHI660E electrochemical analyzer (ChenHua, China).It was operated in a three-electrode system, and a platinum electrode, indium tin oxide glass, and saturated AgAgCl electrode were acted as the counter electrode, working electrode, and standard electrode, respectively.The electrolyte was a 0.1 M sodium sulfate solution.Electrochemical impendence spectroscopes (EIS) was carried out on a PP211 electrochemical analyzer (Zahner, Germany).It was performed in the presence of 5 mM K 3 Fe(CN) 6 K 4 Fe(CN) 6 (1:1) mixture as a redox probe in 0.1 M potassium chloride (KCl) at 0.24 V.

reaction medium.source: Before illumination, 100 mg of photocatalysts were suspended in 100 mL of KHCO 3 (0.1 M) and Na 2 SO 3 (0.1 M) under magnetic stirring in the double-layer Pyrex glass reactor, and pure CO 2 gas was continuously through the solution for 30 min to eliminate air and dissolve oxygen.
reaction medium.context: Melamine, Polyethylene glycol (PEG) 400, cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), ammonia aqueous, NaOH, KHCO 3 , and Na 2 SO 3 were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent.All reagents used in this work were analytically pure and used without further purification.Sodium silicate solution was extracted from rice husk by the alkali solution method.Briefly, 38 g of calcined rice husk ash and 10 g of NaOH were mixed in 350 mL of water, and then stirred and refluxed at 70 C for 24 h.The mixed liquid was filtered, and the Si content in the clear liquid measured by ICP-OES was 39.1 gL. The g-C 3 N 4 nanotubes (NT) were prepared by a two-step process with a hydrothermal performing process.Firstly, 2 g of melamine was dissolved into 120 mL of deionized water under the condition of stirring at 80 C for 10 min.Then, the obtained clear solution was transferred to a 200 mL PTFE-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated to 180 C for 24 h.The crystalline product, preformed white melamine-cyanuric acid (MCA), was collected by filtered, washed several times with water, and completely dried.Secondly, the 3 g of MCA precursor was placed in an alumina crucible with a cover and then calcined at 550 C for h in a tube furnace with N 2 gas flow.For comparison, the bulk g-C 3 N 4 was prepared by thermal polymerization of melamine.Typically, 2 g of melamine was heated at 550 C for 4 h.The light yellow powder was collected after cooled and ground. The TPS was synthesized by the following soft template method.10 g of PEG was dissolved in 40 mL deionized water and stirred for min at 40 C, which labeled as solution A. 1 g of CTAB was dissolved in 10 mL deionized water at 60 C, which labeled as solution B. Solution A was added to solution B, and then 18 mL of sodium silicate solution was added dropwise to the mixed solution after 1 mL of ammonia aqueous was added.The mixture gel was stirred with 300 rmin at 40 C for 8 h.Finally, the product was filtrated, washed, and dried.Then the template was removed by heated to 550 C for 6 h with a ramp rate of 10 Cmin. The TPSg-C 3 N 4 NT composite was prepared by a hydrothermal method.0.3 g of g-C 3 N 4 NT was dispersed in 100 mL of water by ultrasonic treatment for 10 min.0.1 g of TPS was added to the above suspension, and the ultrasonic treatment was continued for 30 min.Then, the mixed solution was transferred to a 200 mL PTFE-lined stainless steel autoclave, which was heated at 120 C for 12 h.Finally, the product was filtrated, washed with deionized water, and dried at 80 C. Samples with the same preparation method but different amounts of TPS, 0.1 g, 0.2 g, and 0.3 g, were labeled TGNT1, TGNT2, and TGNT3. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed using a D8 Advance X-ray powder diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) with Cu K radiation ( 0.154 nm).The pattern data was recorded at 2 5 to 75 with the step size of 0.02 .Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed on a Cary 660 Spectrum One FTIR spectrometer (Agilent Technologies, Australia) in the range of 4000-600 cm -1 .X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data was obtained using an Axis Ultra DLD spectrometer (KRATOS, UK) using monochromatic Al K radiation (h1486.6eV).The signal at 284.8 eV of the C (1s) region was employed to adjust the binding energies for all of the XPS spectra.Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained on an ULTRA PLUS field emission scanning electron microscope (ZEISS, Germany) at an accelerating voltage of 15 kV.Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were observed with a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) working at 200 kV.The N 2 adsorption-desorption isotherm at -196 C and CO 2 adsorption isotherm at 0 C were measured on an ASAP 2460 static adsorption analyzer (Micromeritics, USA).Specific surface area, pore size distribution, and total pore volume were calculated by the Brunauer-Emmet-Teller (BET) method, the non-local density functional theory (NLDFT) method, and the adsorption amount at the relative pressure PP 0 of 0.995.Before each measurement, the samples were degassed at 200 C for 6 h.UV-Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) data was conducted using a Lambda 650S UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA) in the wavelength range of 200-800 nm.Photoluminescence (PL) spectra was recorded using a Fluoromax-4 spectrophotometer (HORIBA, Japan) with an excitation wavelength of 325 nm.
